coredata.dc:title: Strongly coupled carbon quantum dots/NiCo-LDHs nanosheets on carbon cloth as electrode for high performance flexible supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Wansheng
Chen, Wenchao
Quan, Hongying
Zhang, Zhi-Xia
Zeng, Yong
Wang, Yunying
Chen, Dezhi
coredata.dc:description: NiCo-LDHs is one of the most promising electrode materials for supercapacitors. However, the intrinsic low conductivity and the volume expansion/contraction still seriously limit the application of NiCo-LDHs in supercapacitors. Herein, we constructed carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/NiCo-LDHs nanosheets heterostructures on carbon cloth (CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC) through a simple solvothermal method using CQDs-embedded Co-MOF as precursor. The in-situ implanting of CQDs not only can effectively improve the conductivity of the NiCo-LDHs but also boost the long-term structural stability of the flexible electrode through the morphological optimization of NiCo-LDHs. Benefit from the ingenious structural design and strong coupling between CQDs and NiCo-LDHs, the CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC electrode delivered a much-enhanced capacitance performance in 6 M KOH, including a large reversible specific capacitance of 2023 F g−1 (5.22 F cm−2) at 5 mA cm−2, superior rate capability with 84.3% capacitance retention at 30 mA cm−2 and excellent cycle life. The flexible asymmetric supercapacitor device exhibited an energy density of 117.8 Wh kg−1 with a power density of 528.3 W kg−1 at 2 mA cm−2 using CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC as positive electrode and activated CC as negative electrode. This work provides a new opportunity for the development of nanocomposites with the in-situ implanting of CQDs to construct high-performance electrodes for flexible supercapacitors.

head.ce:title: Strongly coupled carbon quantum dots/NiCo-LDHs nanosheets on carbon cloth as electrode for high performance flexible supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanchang Hangkong University, Nanchang 330063, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: NiCo-LDHs is one of the most promising electrode materials for supercapacitors. However, the intrinsic low conductivity and the volume expansion/contraction still seriously limit the application of NiCo-LDHs in supercapacitors. Herein, we constructed carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/NiCo-LDHs nanosheets heterostructures on carbon cloth (CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC) through a simple solvothermal method using CQDs-embedded Co-MOF as precursor. The in-situ implanting of CQDs not only can effectively improve the conductivity of the NiCo-LDHs but also boost the long-term structural stability of the flexible electrode through the morphological optimization of NiCo-LDHs. Benefit from the ingenious structural design and strong coupling between CQDs and NiCo-LDHs, the CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC electrode delivered a much-enhanced capacitance performance in 6 M KOH, including a large reversible specific capacitance of 2023 F g−1 (5.22 F cm−2) at 5 mA cm−2, superior rate capability with 84.3% capacitance retention at 30 mA cm−2 and excellent cycle life. The flexible asymmetric supercapacitor device exhibited an energy density of 117.8 Wh kg−1 with a power density of 528.3 W kg−1 at 2 mA cm−2 using CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC as positive electrode and activated CC as negative electrode. This work provides a new opportunity for the development of nanocomposites with the in-situ implanting of CQDs to construct high-performance electrodes for flexible supercapacitors.

ce:section.ce:para: To realize the multi-function of wearable electronic devices, such as smart glasses, smart jackets, skin patches and AR/VR wearable devices, highly flexible and mechanically stable energy storage systems are required [1]. The emerging of flexible supercapacitors (SCs) integrates the higher power density and excellent cycling-lifespan with conspicuous mechanical properties, has received widespread attention in both scientific and technological communities in recent years [2-4]. The realization of high-performance flexible SCs heavily depends on the electrical properties and mechanical integrity of the constitutive materials and their ingenious assembly into free-standing and binder-less skeleton [5]. However, compared with lithium-ion batteries, current flexible SCs still suffer from relatively low energy density. These drawbacks make flexible SCs hard to meet the ever-growing demand for wearable electronic products [3,6]. One of most effective approaches to enhance the energy density of flexible SCs is to assemble asymmetric supercapacitors (ASCs) using high-capacitive battery-type and capacitor-type materials as positive and negative electrodes, respectively, and the operating voltage window can be significantly broadened by a suitable combination of electrolytes [7-10]. Thus, the design and synthesis of electrode materials with excellent performance have become the focus of the development of flexible SCs.

Among the groups of excellent electrode materials reported, layered double hydroxides (LDHs), particularly, NiCo-LDHs has been regarded as a potential battery-type electrode for flexible SCs and attracted many attentions due to their extremely high theoretical specific capacitance, outstanding redox activity, variable Ni2+/Ni3+ and Co2+/Co3+ oxidation states and fast (de-)intercalation of charged ions [11-14]. However, the intrinsic low conductivity and the volume expansion/contraction still seriously hinder the application of NiCo-LDHs for flexible SCs [15]. These issues can be effectively solved by the controllable and rational design of vertical-standing nanoarray structures [16,17]. An efficient strategy is to construct NiCo-LDHs arrays on the highly conductive and flexible substrate through the self-templated transformation of MOFs with well-defined structure [18,19].

As a typical MOFs with expanded zeolite topologies [20,21], ZIF-67 (Co-MOF) has been used as sacrificial template to construct high active NiCo-LDHs with high specific surface area and adjustable pore size distribution in flexible SCs [22]. Zhong group [23] created ZIF-67 derived three-dimensional NiCo-LDHs material on CC. The fabricated NiCo-LDHs@CC flexible electrode showed a high specific capacitance of 2685 F g−1 in 2 M KOH aqueous electrolyte at 1 A g−1. Xuan et al. [24] constructed a hollow-structured NiCo-LDH on acidified CC. The prepared H-NiCo-LDH@ACC flexible electrode could deliver a promising capacitance of 3060 mF cm−2 in 1 M KOH aqueous electrolyte at 1 mA cm−2. Even though high specific capacitance can be achieved in these fabricated flexible electrodes of NiCo-LDHs@CC, however, NiCo-LDHs still displayed unsatisfactory rate capability and cycle stability owing to the destruction of the interlayer microstructure of LDH, blocking the ion transport channels, reducing the stability of LDH structure and increasing the barrier of ion transfer. Thus, the recent research interests on NiCo-LDHs electrodes have focused on the rational structural design to achieve not only high electrochemical activity but also long-term structure stability.

Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have gained considerable attention in electrode materials for SCs due to their individual electronic properties and ability to carry multiple elements on the surface [25]. Numerous studies have found that CQDs can provide more charge transfer space and paths during charging and discharging, resulting in the improvement of rate capability and long-period stability [26]. Moreover, CQDs were also used as structural stabilizing agent to preserve the surface of metal-compound owing to the unique nanosize effect and surface effect [27-29]. Therefore, introducing CQDs into the NiCo-LDHs can not only keep the integrity interlamellar structure of LDHs, but also greatly reduce the ion transport distance, which should be an effective methodology to improve the electrochemical properties of NiCo-LDHs. Herein, we propose a facile strategy to implant CQDs into NiCo-LDHs arrays to improve the rate capability and cycle stability while maintaining the structural integrity of flexible NiCo-LDHs@CC electrodes. Co-MOF was used as precursor, and CQDs were immobilized into Co-MOF by chemistry bath deposition. CQDs/Co-MOF nanoarrays were in situ uniformly grown on the skeletons of CC. CQDs can effectively embed into the Co-MOF frameworks without affecting their crystallinity and morphology because the small size and abundant oxygen-containing groups. Then the CQDs/Co-MOF nanoarrays were converted into porous CQDs/NiCo-LDHs by solvothermal reaction. Accordingly, the obtained CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC electrode shows the following advantages compare with previous pseudocapacitive materials. First, the obtained electrode presented a well-defined porous nanostructure with abundant active sites and convenient ion diffusion paths, which is favorable for electrolyte enter into the whole nanoarrays, because of the CQDs/NiCo-LDH nanoarrays vertically arranged on the surface of the carbon fiber unit in CC. Second, the introduction of CQDs can improve the structure stability of NiCo-LDH nanoarrays and buffer the huge volume change of active materials during long-term cycling, which is conducive to further boosting the ion transfer efficiency. Moreover, no binder is needed for CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC flexible electrode, which avoids the decrease in conductivity caused by the resistance of the binder and further facilitate the electron transfer of active materials to current collector. With the above advantages, the obtained CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC demonstrated a high specific capacitance of 5.22 F cm−2 and 2023 F g−1 at 5 mA cm−2, and enhanced cycle stability with 83.3% of capacitance retention after 5000 cycles. Meanwhile, an ASC with good flexibility based on CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC electrode was assembled, and a high energy density of 117.8 Wh kg−1 with a power density of 528.3 W kg−1 could be achieved at the current density of 2 mA cm−2. The results show that the CQDs embedding is an effective way to boost the electrochemical properties of Co-MOF and MOFs derived materials. It is believed that the strategy of CQDs embedding is applicable and can be applied for improving the electrochemical performance of other electrodes.

Carbon cloth (CC, WOS1002) was purchased from Taiwan Carbon Energy. CO(NO3)2 6H2O (>98.5%), NiCl2 6H2O (>98%), citric acid (>99.5%), urea (>99%), concentrated nitric acid (HNO3, 65.0 ∼ 68.0%), concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl, 34 ∼ 37%), concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98%) and KMnO4 (99%) were purchased from Shangtou Xilong Scientific. 2-methylimidazole (98%) was acquired from Shanghai Macklin Biochemistry. All chemical reagents were used without further refinement

First, the CC (2 × 3 cm2) was pretreated by N2-plasma, and then treated using concentrated HNO3 at 100 °C for 24 h, followed by washing with deionized water. The Co-MOF and CQDs/Co-MOF arrays were grown on CC by a facile chemical bath deposition method. For a typical preparation, 40 mL of 2-methylimidazole aqueous solution (40 mM) was quickly added into 40 mL of Co(NO3)2·6H2O aqueous solution (50 mM). The treated CC was immersed in the above mixed solution at room temperature for 22 h. The obtained Co-MOF@CC was washed and dried. The CQDs/Co-MOF@CC was obtained by combining 80 mg of CQDs (see Section S1 and Figure S1) in the reaction solution under the same reaction condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of NiCo-LDHs@CC and CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: 0.1585 g of NiCl2·6H2O and 0.0969 g of Co(NO3)2·6H2O was dissolved in 30 mL of methanol, and then the homogeneous solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave containing the Co-MOF@CC or CQDs/Co-MOF@CC formed in the previous step. The autoclave was held at 120 ° C for 1 h to convert Co-MOF@CC into NiCo-LDHs@CC and then naturally cooled. NiCo-LDHs@CC and CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC electrodes were washed with absolute ethanol and dried at 60 °C for 12 h. The mass loading of NiCo-LDHs and CQDs/NiCo-LDHs on CC were about 2.5 mg cm−2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of activated carbon cloth (ACC) electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The N2-plasma treated CC was activated in a mixed solution of 20 mL of concentrated HNO3, 40 mL of concentrated H2SO4 and 6 g of KMnO4 for 24 h at 0 °C. The activated CC (ACC) was washed and soaked in concentrated HCl for 12 h. Finally, the ACC was washed, dried, and used as negative electrode.

The morphology and structure of as-prepared flexible electrodes were observed using FEI Quanta 250 scanning electron microscope (SEM) and FEI Talos F200X transmission electron microscope (TEM). The phase was characterized on a Bruker D8 Advance XRD diffractometer (XRD) with Cu Ka radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). The elements composition of as-prepared samples was determined using inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS, Thermo Fisher, iCAP RQ-MS). The elements and chemical states were analyzed by Kratos Axis Ultra X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was obtained on Bruker VERTEX70 FTIR spectrometer. Thermogravimetric (TG) analysis was carried on a TA TGA 5500 thermogravimetric analyzer in an air atmosphere with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1. Bruker A300 electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectrometer was used to collect the EPR spectra to evaluate the oxygen vacancy. The pore structure of the as-prepared samples was analyzed by N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm at 77 K obtained on surface area & pore size analyzer (Micromeritics TriStar II 3020).

Electrochemical measurements were carried on a Chenhua CHI760e electrochemical workstation using a three-electrode system in 6 M KOH aqueous solution at room temperature (25 ± 2 ° C). Herein, the as-prepared flexible electrode (1 cm × 1 cm), platinum plate (1 cm × 1 cm) and Hg/HgO electrode were used as the working electrode, the counter electrode, and the reference electrode, respectively. Cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were used to evaluate the electrochemical properties of the as-prepared flexible electrodes.

The areal capacitance (C s) of the as-prepared samples in the three-electrode system was calculated according to equation (1), the galvanostatic capacitance was calculated according to equation (2), respectively.

In addition, an asymmetry supercapacitor device (ASC) was assembled using a piece of filter paper as the separator, CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC as positive electrode, ACC as negative electrode, 6 M KOH as the electrolyte, respectively. The ACS was sealed by PVC film, and its thickness was 1.7 mm. Electrochemical properties of the ASC were tested on a CHI760e electrochemical workstation and a CT2001A battery test system at room temperature (25 ± 2 ° C). The energy density and power density of the ASC were calculated through the following equation (3) and (4), respectively.

The preparation process of NiCo-LDHs@CC and CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC electrodes is presented in Scheme 1 . A conventional pre-treatment of N2-plasma and acid treatment for CC was performed to introduce a variety of oxygen-containing functional groups, which can improve the hydrophilicity of the CC and is conducive to the growth of the Co-MOF array. Through a facile chemical bath deposition method, uniform coating of numerous Co-MOF or CQDs/Co-MOF nanoarrays were grown on the surface of CC. Subsequently, the Co-MOF and CQDs/Co-MOF nanoarrays were used as self-sacrificial templates to form NiCo-LDHs@CC and CQDs/NiCo-LDH@CC through solvothermal reaction, respectively. The XRD patterns of the synthesized materials were shown in Fig. 1 a. The board peaks at 25.8° and 42.6° are belonged to the CC. Compared with pure CC, the XRD spectra of the Co-MOF have several peaks, which are match well with the results as previously reported [30,31], suggesting the successful synthesis of Co-MOF@CC. The diffraction pattern of CQDs/Co-MOF@CC is similar to Co-MOF@CC, indicating that the CQDs did not influence the phase of Co-MOF. For the as-prepared NiCo-LDHs@CC and CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC samples, several new diffraction peaks at 11.4°, 22.7°, 33.5°, 34.4°, 38.8° and 60.0° correspond to the (003), (006), (101), (012), (015) and (110) planes of Ni(OH)2·0.75H2O (JCPDS#38–0715), respectively [24]. In addition, the diffraction peaks at 16.2°, 32.0°, 39.3° and 53.6° can be assigned to the (101), (113), (024) and (220) planes of Co2(OH)3Cl, respectively (JCPDS#73–2134) [32]. The composition of NiCo-LDHs and CQDs/NiCo-LDHs were further estimated using an inductively coupled plasma emission spectrometer, the results were listed in Table S1. The ratio of Ni(OH)2·0.75H2O to Co2(OH)3Cl in NiCo-LDHs was calculated to be 1.077, while 1.121 in CQDs/NiCo-LDHs. The XRD results demonstrate that the Co-MOF nanoarrays were converted to NiCo-LDHs through solvothermal reaction. FTIR spectra (Fig. 1b) was measured to further analyze the transformation from Co-MOF to NiCo-LDHs. Compared with the FTIR spectrum of CC, the bands at 432 cm−1 (Co–N stretching), 745 cm−1 (C-H bending out-of-plane of the imidazole ring), 994 cm−1 (C–N stretching), 1072 and 1565 cm−1 (C = N stretching) indicate that the presence of Co-MOF on CC. In addition, there is no obvious difference for the characteristic bands of Co-MOF after the modification of CQDs. After the solvothermal reaction, these bands of Co–N, C–N and C = N groups disappeared, and new frequency bands were identified to the Co-O and Ni-O vibrations at 516 and 422 cm−1 [33,34]. The FT-IR results match well with the previous XRD analysis, further confirming the successful formation of the NiCo-LDHs and CQDs/NiCo-LDHs on CC through the self-sacrificial template of Co-MOF.

Fig. 2 presents SEM images of as-prepared Co-MOF@CC, CQDs/Co-MOF@CC, NiCo-LDHs@CC, and CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC. As shown in Fig. 2a-b, the leaf-like Co-MOF nanoflakes are evenly and vertically arranged on the surface of the carbon fiber unit in CC to form a core-shell structure. Similarly, CQDs/Co-MOF also exhibits the leaf-like morphology on the carbon fibers (Fig. 2c-d), and the thickness of CQDs/Co-MOF shell on carbon fiber is about 5.5 μm (Figure S2). To observe CQDs in Co-MOF nanoflakes, Figure S3 show the TEM image of CQDs/Co-MOF. As can be seen from Figure S3a, CQDs are uniformly embedded in Co-MOF nanoflakes. The HRTEM image of CQDs/Co-MOF in Figure S3b shows clear lattice stripes with spacing of 0.21 nm in Co-MOF nanoflakes, which matches well with the (101) crystal plane of CQDs and further demonstrates that CQDs was embedded in Co-MOF successfully through our synthesis method. Through a facile solvothermal reaction, a uniform coating of NiCo-LDHs nanoflakes with high density was formed on the surface of CC (Fig. 2e) and remained the core-shell structure. In addition, the thickness of NiCo-LDHs shell is less than the Co-MOF precursor. As shown in Fig. 2f, the NiCo-LDHs nanoflakes are thinner and curlier than the Co-MOF nanoflakes, and these NiCo-LDHs nanoflakes possess flocculent surface with hierarchical structure. Fig. 2g-h shows the SEM images of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC. Compared with the bare NiCo-LDHs@CC, the thickness of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs shell is smaller, and the surface of NiCo-LDHs nanoflakes become rougher and display a cactus-like structure. Moreover, the CQDs/NiCo-LDHs nanoflakes exhibit an interlaced structure rather than the scattered form of NiCo-LDHs nanoflakes, further confirming the compact texture of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs on CC, which is beneficial to the stability of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC electrode.

TEM images of Co-MOF-derived NiCo-LDHs were performed to further analyze the structure of as-prepared electrodes and shown in Fig. 3 . As shown in Fig. 3a, these leaf-like NiCo-LDHs nanoflakes (Fig. 2f) are composited of dozens of interconnected sheet-like NiCo-LDHs subunits and show a compact feature (Fig. 3b). Moreover, the transparent feature of the NiCo-LDHs nanosheets in Fig. 3b further indicates its ultrathin nature. HRTEM image shown in Fig. 3c taken from the labeled area in Fig. 3b presents resolved lattice fringes with the spacings of 0.25 and 0.22 nm, which can be assigned to the (018) plane of Ni(OH)2·0.75H2O (JCPDS#38–0715) and the (024) plane of Co2(OH)3Cl (PDF#73–2134), respectively. In addition, the EDS mapping (Fig. 3d) of the NiCo-LDHs nanoflakes in Fig. 3b reveal that the Ni, Co, O and Cl are uniformly distributed in NiCo-LDHs nanosheets. As shown in the TEM image of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs in Fig. 3e, CQDs/NiCo-LDHs nanosheets are tightly connected to form a hierarchical structure, indicating that the in-situ implanting of CQDs could tune the interlacement of NiCo-LDHs nanosheets, which will improve the ion diffusion and the stability of electrode. In addition, abundant mesopores can be seen in the hierarchical structure of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs (Fig. 3f). To further prove the existence of mesoporous in CQDs/NiCo-LDHs, the N2 gas adsorption/desorption isotherms were analyzed using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) technique. The BET specific surface area and porous nature of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs and NiCo-LDHs were presented in Figure S4. The isothermal plots with hysteresis loop between adsorption and desorption in the P/P0 range of 0.5–1.0 (Figure S4a) indicates the presence of mesopores within the samples. The calculated specific surface area of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs and NiCo-LDHs are about 14.47 and 15.75 m2 g−1, respectively. The pore size distribution curves in Figure S4b confirm the mesoporous nature of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs. The pore volume of NiCo-LDH and CQDs/NiCo-LDHs are 0.079 and 0.115 cm3 g−1, respectively. The higher SBET and pore volume mean the higher active sites and more convenient channel of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs for energy storage. This structural feature can be very favorable by providing more exposure to active sites on the electrode and assisting the diffusion of electrolytes. The clear lattice fringes observed from the HRTEM image of the CQDs/NiCo-LDHs nanosheets present spacing of 0.25 and 0.29 nm (Fig. 3g), which corresponds to the (018) plane of Ni(OH)2·0.75H2O (JCPDS#38–0715) and the (220) plane of Co2(OH)3Cl (PDF#73–2134), respectively, and in accordance with the XRD diffraction patterns, suggesting that the structure of NiCo-LDH was maintained during the in-situ implanting of CQDs. Several tiny nanocrystallines of CQDs with lattice spacing of 0.21 nm can also be seen on the edges of the NiCo-LDHs nanosheets, indicating the good anchor-hold between CQDs and NiCo-LDH, which could significantly promote active site exposure. CQDs with high crystallinity and ultra-small size will facilitate the diffusion of ion toward the surface of electrode, thus improves its electrochemical activity [35]. A combined study of high-angle annular dark field (HAADF) imaging and EDS mapping (Fig. 3h) of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs nanosheets shows that Ni, Co, O, Cl and C elements are homogeneously distributed. TG curves (Figure S5) show that the mass percentage of CQDs in CQDs/NiCo-LDHs is 2.25%.

To further analyze the conversion from Co-MOF to NiCo-LDHs and the interaction between CQDs and NiCo-LDHs, XPS spectroscopy was used to characterize the as-prepared samples and shown in Fig. 4 . Co, O, C, N elements in Co-MOF@CC and CQDs/Co-MOF@CC can be clearly observed, and Ni and Cl signals appear in the obtained NiCo-LDHs@CC and CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC after solvothermal-treatment (Fig. 4a). In the high-resolution XPS spectra of Ni 2p (Figure S6a), Ni 2p1/2 (855.1 eV) and Ni 2p3/2 (872.7 eV) peaks accompanied with two distinct satellites peaks (denoted as “Sat.”) at 861.0 and 878.8 eV can be ascribed to the Ni2+. The spin-energy distance between the two prominent peaks is 17.6 eV, which further confirms the presence of Ni2+ in NiCo-LDH@CC [36]. The binding energy of Ni 2p does not change obviously in CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC. Fig. 4b displays the Co 2p spectra of as-prepared samples. Two significantly split spin-orbit peaks located at around 781 (Co 2p3/2) and 797 (Co 2p1/2) with two obvious satellite peaks at around 786 and 802 eV suggest the presence of Co2+ in the Co-MOF. In addition, the spin-energy distance of Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2 in Co-MOF is 15.5 eV, further indicating the presence of Co2+ rather than Co3+. After solvothermal treatment, two doublet peaks of Co 2p3/2 (the binding energies: 780.4 and 782.4 eV) and Co 2p1/2 (the binding energies: 795.8 and 793.3 eV) at 780.4/795.8 eV and 782.4/793.3 eV in NiCo-LDHs@CC are attributed to Co3+ and Co2+, respectively. The decreased binding energy of Co 2p means that the Co-N in Co-MOF was converted to Co-OH in LDHs. In addition, the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of Co 2p3/2 increased from 2.62 to 3.02 eV and the satellite peak intensity decreased, which also suggests the coexistence of Co2+ and Co3+ in the surface NiCo-LDHs. The presence of Co3+ is convenient to the energy storage in NiCo-LDHs. Notably, the binding energies of Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2 in NiCo-LDHs@CC and CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC were lower than that of Co-MOF@CC and CQDs/Co-MOF@CC respectively, suggesting the double peaks of Co 2p in Co-MOF@CC and CQDs/Co-MOF@CC shift to the low binding energy after the solvothermal treatment. The shift of binding energy, that is, the decrease of Co 2p binding energy, suggesting an increase in the electron density of NiCo-LDHs, which could be attributed to the transfer of some electrons from the electron-rich structure of CQDs to NiCo-LDHs as reported in previous literature [37,38]. Moreover, the shift of binding energy also reflects the strong electronic interaction between CQDs and NiCo-LDHs, which is helpful to the charge transport and ion diffusion during the electrochemical process. Figure S6b shows the Cl spectra of CQDs/Co-MOFs@CC and CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC, indicating the presence of Cl in Co-MOFs and NiCo-LDHs. Fig. 4c presents the O 1 s XPS spectra of as-prepared samples. O 1 s XPS spectra of Co-MOF can be fitted to two species of C = O (531.9 eV) and C-O (532.6 eV), which are originated from the oxygen-containing groups on the surface of CC after pre-treatment of N2-plasma and acid treatment. The abundant C = O groups at the edge of CQDs increased the ratio of C = O in CQDs/Co-MOF@CC. The appearance peak of 529.8 eV is attributed to the metal-O group. The increased proportion of C = O and the corresponding decreased C-O in NiCo-LDHs@CC and CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC indicate that partial of C-O groups was oxidized to C = O after solvothermal reaction. Similarly, the O 1 s peaks of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC also move to low binding energy, confirming the unique chemical interaction between CQDs and NiCo-LDHs. Furthermore, the content of metal-O is decreased in CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC, indicating that the in-situ implanting of CQDs could increase the oxygen vacancy in NiCo-LDHs. As shown in Figure S7, a strong electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) signal at g = 2.002 is related to the oxygen vacancy [39,40]. The increased signal intensity of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs indicating more oxygen vacancy after in-situ implanting of CQDs, which is helped to improve the electrochemical performance of electrode materials. The high-resolution C 1 s spectra are presented in Fig. 4d. The deconvoluted C 1 s XPS spectra of Co-MOF showed four species, including C-C (285.8 eV), C-O/C-N (285.7 eV), C = O (286.8 eV) and π-π* satellite (292.0 eV), which originated from the organic ligand in Co-MOF and CC. After introduction of CQDs, the content of C-O increased in CQDs/Co-MOF@CC. For the as-prepared NiCo-LDHs@CC, three species of C-C (284.8 eV), C-O (286.2 eV) and C = O (288.0 eV) are attributed to the CC. After modification of CQDs, the content of C = O in CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC is increased, implying that the CQDs were successfully in-situ implanted in NiCo-LDHs, and partial of C-O in CQDs were oxidized to C = O during the solvothermal reaction.

The electrochemical performances of CC, Co-MOF@CC, CQDs/Co-MOF@CC, NiCo-LDHs@CC and CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC electrodes were evaluated via a three-electrode system in 6 M KOH at room temperature. Fig. 5 a shows the CV curves of CC, Co-MOF@CC, CQDs/Co-MOF@CC, NiCo-LDHs@CC and CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC electrodes at the scanning speed of 5 mV s−1. The straight line of CV curve of bare CC electrode indicates that its capacitance is almost negligible. The electrochemical activities of Co-MOF@CC and CQDs/Co-MOF@CC electrodes are also deficient. The integrated area of CV curves for NiCo-LDHs@CC and CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC electrodes is significantly larger than Co-MOF@CC and CQDs/Co-MOF@CC. Besides, the apparent redox peaks in NiCo-LDHs@CC and CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC electrodes indicate the characteristic of battery-type capacitance due to the following redox reactions in the alkaline electrolyte (5)-(6) [24]:

Fig. 5b displays the GCD curves of CC, Co-MOF@CC, CQDs/Co-MOF@CC, NiCo-LDHs@CC and CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC electrodes under the current density of 5 mA cm−2. No obvious potential platform and short GCD time indicate that the CC, Co-MOF@CC and CQDs/Co-MOF@CC electrodes possess poor capacitive properties. By contrast, the NiCo-LDHs@CC and CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC electrodes exhibit two potential platforms around 0.2 V vs. Hg/HgO and appreciable GCD time, suggesting a typical battery-type capacitance. In addition, according to the CV area and the discharge time, CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC show higher specific capacitance than NiCo-LDHs@CC. To further explore electrochemical performance of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC and NiCo-LDHs@CC, CV curves were obtained under different scan rates of 1 ∼ 10 mV s−1 and shown in Fig. 5c and Figure S8a. The current response in CV curves increases with the increasing of scan rate and remains the original shape. According to the CV curves, the related calibration curves (R2 = 1) between the scan rate and the peak current are shown in the Figure S9a, and good linear relationship reveals that the energy storage reaction of the NiCo-LDHs@CC and NiCo/CQDs-LDH@CC electrodes is predominated by diffusion-controlled electrochemical process [41]. To confirm the contribution of battery-type capacitance in electrochemical reactions, the diffusion-controlled contribution and capacitive contribution of the NiCo-LDHs@CC and NiCo/CQDs-LDH@CC electrodes were analyzed based on the Power-law (Section S2), respectively [42]. As shown in Figure S9b, the b values were obtained by the slope of a linear fitting line of log(υ) vs. log(i) from the CV curves. When the b value is 1, it represents capacitive-type electrochemical behavior, and when the b value is 0.5, it represents the diffusion-controlled charge storage behavior. From the redox peak current, the b value of NiCo-LDHs@CC and CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC is 0.66 and 0.63, respectively. It shows that the battery-type oxidation-reduction kinetics is dominant, and its charge storage capacitances is controlled by slow semi-infinite diffusion[41,43]. The lower b value of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC imply that the semi-infinite diffusion reaction of the CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC electrode is more obvious and controllable than that of NiCo-LDHs@CC. As shown in Figure S10 and S11, the contribution proportion of the diffusion-control and capacitance-control of the electrochemical process was further calibrated according to the modified power-law [41]. From Fig. 5d, the diffusion-controlled contribution proportion of the CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC is higher than that of the NiCo-LDHs@CC electrode as the scanning rate increases from 1 mV s−1 to 10 mV s−1, suggesting that the strong coupling between CQDs and NiCo-LDHs is beneficial to facilitates the reaction kinetics. At the scanning rate of 1 mV s−1, the CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC presented much higher diffusion-controlled behavior (70.9%) compared to the capacitive contribution (29.1%), indicates that the redox reactions dominate the charge storage process. In addition, the diffusion-controlled contribution decreases to 43.2%, while capacitive contribution progressively increases to 56.8% as the scanning rate increases to 10 mV s−1. These results collectively verify the intrinsic electrochemical activity CQDs. Generally, the diffusion-controlled behavior cannot achieve fast ion transfer to satisfy the rapid electrochemical reactions at high scan rate [44]. The capacitive behavior of in-situ implanting of CQDs near the surface of electrodes compensate rapid electro-kinetics, which enables fast ion migration and efficiency redox reaction. The battery-type capacitance was further investigated through GCD tests. Fig. 5e and Figure S8b demonstrate the GCD curves of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC and NiCo-LDHs@CC in the voltage range of 0 ∼ 0.5 V at different current densities, respectively. The non-linear triangular charge–discharge plots enlighten the battery-type behavior of NiCo-LDHs. The calculated areal and gravimetric specific capacitance of NiCo-LDHs@CC and CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC based on discharge curves are exhibited in Fig. 5f. The areal and gravimetric capacities of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC were 5.22 F cm−2 and 2023 F g−1 at 5 mA cm−2, which is higher than that of NiCo-LDHs@CC (3.36 F cm−2 and 1318 F g−1). It is worth emphasizing that the CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC deliver excellent rate capability. Even the current density reached up to 30 mA cm−2, the specific capacitance of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC remains 84.3%, much better than that of NiCo-LDHs@CC electrode (69.9%). The above impressive results corroborate the positive effect of in-situ implanting of CQDs on the electrochemical activity of NiCo-LDHs. To better understand the ion transfer and interaction of CQDs and NiCo-LDHs in the CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC, EIS measurements were performed at the frequency range of 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz. The obtained Nyquist plots are presented in Fig. 5g based on the corresponding equivalent circuit (Figure S12). Arc in the high and medium frequency and straight lines in the low frequency appear in all curves. The simulated equivalent circuit and corresponding parameters (the ohmic resistance, R s; charge-transfer resistance, R ct) are listed in Table S2. The CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC has lower Rs and Rct than NiCo-LDHs@CC, suggesting that the CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC electrode delivers the rapid electron transfer. The in-situ implanting of CQDs reduces the charge-transfer and internal resistance of the NiCo-LDHs@CC electrode, which is conducive to fast ion diffusion [45,46]. Cycle stability of the NiCo-LDHs@CC and CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC electrodes were further investigated using continuous GCD test at 30 mA cm−2 and resultant plots were shown in Fig. 5h and Figure S8c. CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC electrode exhibits superior cycling stability, and the capacitance retention is 83.1% after 5 000 cycles, while that of the NiCo-LDHs@CC is 53.7%, confirming the positive effect of in-situ implanting of CQDs on the long-term stability of the NiCo-LDHs@CC.

To further prove the positive effect of CQDs on the cycle stability of the NiCo-LDHs@CC, SEM images of NiCo-LDHs@CC and CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC electrodes after 5000 cycles are presented in Fig. 6 . As shown in Fig. 6a-c, the structure of NiCo-LDHs nanoflakes became looser and suffered a severe deformation after 5 000 cycles compared with the original NiCo-LDHs@CC (Fig. 2e-f), some cracks (white arrow) appear in the NiCo-LDHs and the vertical NiCo-LDHs nanoflake arrays became curly coral-like structures, corresponding with its bad cycling structural stability. Impressively, after 5 000 cycles, the microstructure of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC was kept well without evident destruction, as shown in Fig. 6d-f, indicating the robust structure and excellent cycling stability of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC. Furthermore, the CQDs/NiCo-LDHs nanoflakes became thinner and formed perfect and compact cactus-like nano pillarstructure, which wrapped tightly onto the surface of CC. The morphological changes of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs nanoflakes are ascribed to the induced effect of CQDs. Namely, because of the negative charge of the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CQDs, strong electrostatic interaction occurred between the metal ions and CQDs, which was conducive to the nucleation and growth of nanosheets driven by their inherent layered structure. The strong electrostatic interaction induced them to combine with each other, yielding the compact cactus-like pillarstructure. TEM images were obtained to prove the structure stability of the CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC electrode. Fig. 7 presents the TEM and the EDS results of NiCo-LDHs@CC and CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC after 5000 cycles. Fig. 7a-c show the TEM images of NiCo-LDHs nanoflakes. The curl nanosheets are loosely stacked together to form the NiCo-LDHs nanoflakes (Fig. 7a-b). The loose NiCo-LDHs nanoflakes is easy to exfoliate from the carbon fiber, resulting in poor structure and cycling stability. The clear lattice stripes (Fig. 7c) with lattice spacing of 0.22 and 0.32 nm are attributed to (024) plane of Co2(OH)3Cl (PDF#73–2134) and the (110) plane of Ni(OH)2·0.75H2O (JCPDS#38–0715), respectively. The element EDS mapping (Fig. 7d) indicates that the Ni, Co, O and Cl are evenly distributed in the nanoflake after 5 000 cycles. Fig. 7e-g demonstrate the TEM images of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs nanopillar subunit. The nanopillar is also comprised of curled CQDs/NiCo-LDHs nanosheets. The CQDs/NiCo-LDHs nanosheets are stacked together to form a nanopillar structure. The nanopillar are staggered and tightly wrapped on the surface of carbon fiber like rattan, which is not easy to exfoliate from the carbon fiber, leading robust structure and cycling stability. Fig. 7g shows the HRTEM images of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs nanosheets, the clear lattice stripes with spacing of 0.21 nm is attributed to the CQDs, indicating the stable presence of CQDs during 5000 cycles. In addition, the lattice spacing of 0.22 and 0.19 nm assigned to the (024) plane of Co2(OH)3Cl (PDF#73–2134) and (012) plane Ni(OH)2·0.75H2O (JCPDS#38–0715), respectively. The HAADF-STEM image (Fig. 7i) of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs nanopillar and the corresponding EDS mapping images further confirm the robust durability of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs after 5 000 cycles.

Based on the above experimental results and analysis, the high capacitance, excellent rate capability and structure stability of the CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC electrode are greatly related to the morphological characteristics of the in-situ implanting of CQDs and the strong coupling between NiCo-LDHs nanosheet and CQDs, as schematically presented in Fig. 5i. First of all, the NiCo-LDH provides a secure matrix for holding Ni and Co units with a uniform arrangement, affording an ameliorative electronic structure and interfacial characteristics, thereby increasing the electrochemically active capacitance. Secondly, the strong coupling between NiCo-LDHs nanosheet and CQDs through chemical bonding can construct an overall electron transfer channel, which promotes the reaction kinetics. At last, CQDs have inherent mechanical stability and can be used as structural stabilizers to buffer the deformation of the structure during long-term cycling, which significantly promotes the electrochemical properties and structural stability of the electrode. In addition, the unique hierarchical architecture of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC enables exposure of sufficient active sites in the interconnected network, which accelerates ion migration and redox reactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC based ASC
ce:section.ce:para: Compared with conventional SCs, a flexible device is expected to have desirable advantages, including easy operation, scalability, flexibility, improved safety. To further evaluate the application potential of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC in SCs, flexible asymmetric supercapacitors (ASC) were assembled using CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC, activated CC (ACC) and 6 M KOH as positive electrode, negative electrode and electrolyte. The electrochemical properties of ACC are presented in Figure S13. The ACC exhibits great electric double-layer capacitance between −1.1 and 0 V. The calculated areal capacitance of ACC can reach up to 2.7 F cm−2 at 5 mA cm−2 and maintained 1.9 F cm−2 at 50 mA cm−2 (Figure S13c), showing a high capacitance retention of 70.6%. Fig. 8 a and Fig. 8b present the CV curves of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC//ACC ASC at 50 mV s−1 and GCD curves at 5 mA cm−2 in diverse operating voltage windows, respectively. When the voltage window reaches 1.7 V, the CV curve was polarized due to the occurrence of the oxygen evolution reaction. Therefore, the optimum voltage window for stable operation of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC//ACC ASC was 0–1.6 V. Fig. 8c exhibits the CV profiles of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC//ACC ASC at the scanning rates from 5 to 50 mV s−1, indicating that there are both electric double-layer capacitance and battery-type capacitance at 0–1.6 V. Furthermore, the geometry of the CV curve has no obvious change, proves the outstanding rate capability of the assembled ASC. Fig. 8d presents the GCD profiles of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC//ACC ASC, and the calculated specific capacities (Fig. 8e) of the CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC@CC//ACC ASC were 331.4 F g−1 (0.86 F cm−2) at 2 mA cm−2 and remained 175.2 F g−1 (0.45 F cm−2) at 20 mA cm−2. As shown in Fig. 8f, the CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC//ACC ASC can reach a capacity retention of 86.3% and a coulombic efficiency of 98.1% after 5 000 cycles, suggesting good stability. The ragone plot (Fig. 8g) shows the calculated of the energy (E) and power density (P) of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC@CC//ACC ASC according to the discharge curves. The maximum energy density of CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC//ACC ASC was 117.8 Wh kg−1 with the power density of 528.3 W kg−1 at 2 mA cm−2, and still retained 62.3 Wh kg−1 with 4488 W kg−1 at 20 mA cm−2, which are superior than most recently reported ASC (Table S3) based on LDH, such as Ni-Co-O//NPC-HSC (69 Wh kg−1 at 840 W kg−1) [47], Ni(OH)2/NCDs//3DEG (34.6 Wh kg−1 at 700 W kg−1) [26], α-Co/Ni(OH)2@CQDs-2.5//PPD/rGO (375 Wh kg−1 at 57.26 W kg−1) [48], NiCo-LDH/CFC//AC/CFC HSC (64.9 Wh kg−1 at 804.5 W kg−1) [23], CF@NiCo-A-S//FexOy@CNS (48.2 Wh kg−1 at 840 W kg−1) [49], NiCo-LDH//AC ASC (62.9 Wh kg−1 at 800 W kg−1) [50], Co(OH)F@NiCo-LDH//RGO ASC (49.34 Wh kg−1 at 865 W kg−1)[51], and NiCo-LDH@rGO//AC ASC (32.2 Wh kg−1 at 235.5 W kg−1)[52]. Under the current density of 20 mA cm−2, the CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC//ACC ASC exhibits high cycle stability. In addition, two CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC//ACC ASCs were connected in series can light up light-emitting-diodes (LEDs) successfully (Fig. 8h), and the corresponding video is displayed in Video S1, suggesting the potential as a promising energy storage device. The flexibility properties of the ASC device were conducted by bending the ASC to various bending states (denoted as bent 1-120°, 2-90° and 3-60°), as shown inset of Fig. 8i. The almost overlapping CV curves indicate that the high flexibility of ASC. The above results show that the CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC electrode possesses broad application prospects in the field of portable/wearable electronics due to its high energy density, good cycle stability and flexibility.

In summary, the in-situ implanting of CQDs into NiCo-LDH nanosheets onto the surface of CC was achieved by a self-sacrificing template method using CQDs embed Co-MOF as precursors. In the as-prepared CQDs/NiCo-LDH@CC electrode, the NiCo-LDH plays the role of electrochemically active species while the CQDs serves as structural stabilizing agent to solve the problem of large volume change of NiCo-LDH nanosheets in long-term cycling. The in-situ implanting of CQDs and strong coupling between NiCo-LDHs nanosheet and CQDs can effectively construct the overall electron transfer channel and thus promotes the reaction kinetics, which is conducive to the desirable rate capability and cycle stability. The obtained CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC electrode with stable structure and the desired morphology not only maintains the merits of NiCo-LDHs, but also integrates the superiority of CQDs, which endow the composite electrode with significantly improved electrochemical performance compared with the NiCo-LDH@CC electrode. Electrochemical tests revealed that the CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC delivered an extraordinary specific capacitance (5.22 F cm−2 (2023 F g−1) at 5 mA cm−2) with excellent rate capability and cycling stability. In addition, the assembled asymmetric CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CC//ACC SCs device exhibited an energy density of 117.8 W h kg−1 at a power density of 528.3 W kg−1. These results suggest that our intriguing strategy toward robust structure and excellent capacitive performance enabled by the strong coupling of CQDs and NiCo-LDH electrode materials presents broad application prospect for flexible energy storage devices and can be readily expand to other electrode materials.
